title: Learned Semantic-ID Codebooks via RQ-Kmeans (OneRec / OneRec Technical Report lineage)
authors: Jiaxin Deng, Shiyao Wang, Kuo Cai, Lejian Ren, Qigen Hu, Weifeng Ding, Qiang Luo, Guorui Zhou, et al. (Kuaishou Technology) — OneRec (arXiv:2502.18965, Feb 2025) and OneRec Technical Report (arXiv:2506.13695, Jun 2025)
venue: arXiv:2506.13695 (OneRec Technical Report, primary source for RQ-Kmeans); lineage origin arXiv:2502.18965 (OneRec, cs.IR). NOTE: the URL supplied in the task (arXiv:2406.13235) is a DIFFERENT, unrelated paper (GAL-Rec, graph-aware LLM recs) and does NOT describe RQ-Kmeans — corrected to the OneRec sources during deep-read.
tokenization_method: Semantic IDs via RQ-Kmeans (Residual-Quantized K-means). Each ITEM (short video) is first turned into a single collaborative-aware multimodal embedding: video signals (caption, tag, ASR, OCR, cover image + 5 frames) are encoded by a miniCPM-V-8B vision-language model into ~1280 token vectors (dim 512), compressed to 4 learnable query tokens via a 4-layer QFormer cross-attention, then aligned across collaboratively-similar item pairs with a contrastive loss + caption-generation loss so the embedding carries both content and collaborative signal. That single fused embedding M~ is then discretized by residual quantization with Lt=3 layers, BUT unlike RQ-VAE the codebooks are NOT learned by gradient descent: at layer 1 the codebook is the set of Nt cluster centroids from running K-means over all item embeddings; each item is assigned its nearest centroid; the residual (embedding minus assigned centroid) is computed; layer 2 runs K-means over those residuals to get its codebook; and so on for layer 3. Codebook size Nt = 8192 per layer (ablated up to 32768). The chosen centroid indices (s1, s2, s3) form a coarse-to-fine 3-tuple of discrete codewords = the item's Semantic ID. So an extreme-cardinality (billion-scale) item space is tokenized as a short ordered tuple of learned discrete codes whose codebooks are k-means centroids rather than VQ-VAE-learned vectors.
feature_types_handled: In its native domain: a single fused ITEM-level multimodal+collaborative embedding (text caption/tag/ASR/OCR + image frames, already collapsed into one ~512-d vector by miniCPM-V + QFormer + contrastive alignment). The RQ-Kmeans quantizer itself sees only ONE continuous vector per item — it does NOT operate per-feature, per-column, or on raw heterogeneous tabular fields. All heterogeneity (multimodal, collaborative) is pushed upstream into the encoder/alignment stage. No native numerical/categorical/temporal/missing typing inside the quantizer; mixed inputs only insofar as the upstream encoder fuses them.
token_granularity: learned code, hierarchical. One item -> a tuple of Lt=3 discrete codewords drawn from per-layer codebooks (8192 each). Coarse-to-fine: layer 1 = coarsest semantic cluster, layer 3 = finest residual refinement. Multi-token-per-entity scheme. Same granularity option as TIGER's RQ-VAE semantic IDs, but codebooks are k-means centroids on residuals, not gradient-learned codebook vectors.
numerical_strategy: No dedicated numerical-feature embedding. There are no raw scalar tabular features; the only continuous object is the holistic multimodal embedding, which is discretized via nearest-centroid assignment. No piecewise-linear / periodic / quantile numeric encoding — the discretization is of a fused embedding, not of individual continuous features.
categorical_strategy: No per-field categorical embedding table. The atomic item-ID (which in classic recsys would be an extreme-cardinality categorical with its own giant embedding table) is REPLACED by 3 shared k-means codebooks of 8192 codes each, so billions of items share a small fixed vocabulary and similar items share prefix codes. This is the conceptual inverse of entity-embeddings: shared hierarchical codes instead of one row per category value.
high_cardinality_handling: This is the central transfer hook. A billion-scale, ever-growing item space (atomic IDs infeasible) is mapped to only Lt x Nt = 3 x 8192 = 24576 codeword tokens. RQ-Kmeans turns near-unbounded cardinality into a fixed small vocabulary with coarse-to-fine structure; new/rare items inherit sensible codes via their content embedding's nearest centroids (graceful OOV), and the combinatorial code space Nt^Lt (8192^3) far exceeds the item count so codes can be near-unique. The PAPER'S KEY CLAIM is that k-means-on-residuals gives BETTER codebook utilization and balance than RQ-VAE (RQ-VAE's known codebook-collapse / dead-code failure mode), reported as ~perfect utilization (1.0000) per layer and higher per-layer code entropy than RQ-VAE.
temporal_handling: None inside the tokenizer. Temporality lives at the sequence level only: a user's chronologically ordered watch history is a sequence of semantic-ID tuples fed to the encoder; the decoder generates the next session's items as semantic-ID tuples. RQ-Kmeans has no time axis, no patching, no static/time-varying fusion.
scales_to_many_features: Does NOT demonstrate scaling in the NUMBER OF heterogeneous input FEATURES (this topic's 70+-feature axis). It scales in the NUMBER OF ITEMS (billion-scale corpus cardinality) and in generative-retrieval/serving cost (decode 3 tokens vs dot-product over N items). The quantizer input is always ONE fused embedding regardless of how many raw modalities/signals fed the upstream encoder, so per-feature scaling is simply not tested. Scaling evidence is orthogonal to the 70+-feature question.
downstream_model: An encoder-decoder Transformer with sparse Mixture-of-Experts (OneRec), trained for session-wise next-item generation (generate a whole session of recommended items, not one). Vocabulary = the 3 x 8192 semantic-ID codeword tokens; the decoder autoregressively generates each item as a 3-tuple of codes (beam search), and a reward model + Iterative Preference Alignment (DPO-style) further tunes generation. The semantic IDs are the generation targets that make billion-scale generative retrieval tractable.
pretraining_objective: Tokenizer stage is NON-gradient and self-supervised: K-means (Lloyd's algorithm) over embeddings then residuals, layer by layer — there is no reconstruction/commitment loss like RQ-VAE; the 'training' is just clustering. The upstream multimodal encoder is trained with contrastive alignment (pull together collaboratively-similar item pairs) + caption-generation loss BEFORE quantization. Codes are FROZEN before the generative encoder-decoder is trained with next-item / session-generation objectives. There is NO recommendation-target-aware supervision of the codes themselves (codes capture content+collaborative similarity, not the downstream reward directly).
key_results: (1) Tokenizer ablation: RQ-Kmeans reported to beat RQ-VAE on codebook utilization (~1.0000 per layer vs lower for RQ-VAE) and per-layer code entropy (reported improvements of ~+6.31% / +3.50% / +1.44% for layers 1/2/3) and reconstruction balance — i.e. better-utilized, more balanced codes (numbers from the OneRec Technical Report ablation; could not re-confirm the exact table cells via the HTML excerpt, hence medium confidence). (2) Codebook scaling 8K->32K: long-view rate 0.5118->0.5245 (+2.48%), P-Score 0.2516->0.2635 (+4.75%). (3) Adding collaborative-aware features to a vid-only baseline: P-Score +28.88%, long-view rate +11.34%. (4) Deployment on Kuaishou: original OneRec reports +1.6% watch-time in the main scene; the Technical Report reports App Stay Time +0.54% (Kuaishou) to +1.24% (Kuaishou Lite) with OneRec handling ~25% of queries, attributing gains to the end-to-end system (encoder-decoder + reward), not tokenization alone.
baselines_compared: Tokenizer: RQ-Kmeans vs RQ-VAE (the headline tokenization comparison). System: OneRec generative recommender vs Kuaishou's conventional multi-stage retrieve-then-rank cascade (online A/B). Feature ablations: vid-only vs collaborative-aware multimodal embeddings; codebook-size sweep 8K vs 32K.
transferable_idea: Replace gradient-learned RQ-VAE codebooks with RQ-KMEANS: build each residual-quantization layer's codebook by running k-means on the residuals from the previous layer (Lloyd centroids = codebook; nearest-centroid = assignment). For tokenizing an extreme-cardinality entity (a high-cardinality categorical value, a whole feature-row embedding, or a temporal patch) into a short hierarchical code tuple, k-means-on-residuals is a cheap, training-free quantizer that empirically gives near-perfect codebook utilization and avoids RQ-VAE's codebook-collapse — directly attacking the dead-code risk flagged in the TIGER/RQ-VAE record. For 70+ heterogeneous features: (a) bound high-cardinality vocabulary by mapping each field's value embeddings to shared k-means semantic codes with graceful OOV; (b) compress a whole 70-feature row embedding into a few RQ-Kmeans codes to shorten the transformer sequence — using k-means rather than a second VQ-VAE training stage.
limitations: (1) The quantizer never sees raw heterogeneous features — it sees ONE pre-fused embedding produced by a heavy multimodal encoder (miniCPM-V-8B + QFormer + contrastive alignment); RQ-Kmeans is the easy 10% and the embedding pipeline is the hard 90%. (2) K-means codebooks are FIXED after clustering: they cannot adapt to the downstream generative loss (same non-adaptivity critique as RQ-VAE's frozen codes). (3) K-means must be (re)run as the corpus grows / drifts; centroids are static between refits, raising a maintenance cost for an 'ever-growing' inventory. (4) RQ-Kmeans is described, not exhaustively isolated — most reported online gains come from the full end-to-end system (encoder-decoder MoE + reward + preference alignment), so attributing watch-time lift to the tokenizer is unsupported. (5) Built for billion-item generative RETRIEVAL, not tabular prediction.
pitfalls: 1) SCOPE TRAP (topic anti-pattern): this is a recsys-native generative recommender; importing it wholesale is out of scope. ONLY the RQ-Kmeans 'k-means on residuals as codebook' idea transfers; the MoE encoder-decoder, session-generation, reward model, and DPO alignment are recsys-specific. 2) The headline RQ-Kmeans>RQ-VAE utilization/entropy numbers (1.0000 utilization; +6.31/+3.50/+1.44% entropy) are the most-cited claim but I could NOT re-confirm the exact table cells via the fetched HTML (the table truncated) — treat as medium-confidence, verify against the PDF Section 2.1.2 ablation before citing as fact. 3) Better codebook UTILIZATION/ENTROPY is a tokenizer-internal metric; it is NOT evidence of better DOWNSTREAM tabular accuracy — do not equate the two. 4) The reported online watch-time/stay-time gains are for the whole OneRec system on Kuaishou short-video; they say NOTHING about tabular-prediction accuracy and must not be cited as tokenizer evidence. 5) IMPORTANT METADATA TRAP: the task-supplied arXiv URL 2406.13235 is the WRONG paper (GAL-Rec); the real RQ-Kmeans source is OneRec 2502.18965 / Technical Report 2506.13695. 6) K-means quantization is lossless only up to centroid resolution; two distinct items can map to the same 3-tuple (collision), needing disambiguation as in TIGER.
relevance_to_topic: High-value but narrow, and complementary to the existing TIGER/RQ-VAE record: it is the canonical demonstration that swapping RQ-VAE's learned codebooks for k-means-on-residuals fixes codebook collapse and gives near-perfect, balanced codebook utilization — the single most actionable upgrade to the discretization-vq / recsys-tokenization-transfer route for bounding high-cardinality vocabulary among 70+ mixed features, while the generative-retrieval system around it stays out of scope.
code_available: no (no official RQ-Kmeans release tied to OneRec; OneRec model weights not open). RQ-Kmeans is trivially re-implementable from standard k-means primitives (scikit-learn / faiss) applied layer-by-layer to residuals; community RQ-VAE recsys repos (e.g. EdoardoBotta/RQ-VAE-Recommender) can be adapted by replacing the VQ-VAE codebook update with a k-means refit.
